claim: In participants aged 60 to 80 years with age-related macular degeneration in the Age-Related Eye Disease Study, smoking and hypertension were associated with a higher likelihood of age-related macular degeneration, supporting smoking avoidance and hypertension prevention as potential strategies to reduce risk.
support_summary: Several studies support smoking as a risk factor for age-related macular degeneration (AMD), including a cohort of 60- to 80-year-olds in Copenhagen where smokers who inhaled had a 2.5-fold higher risk of atrophic maculopathy (PMID: 1557977), the Beaver Dam Eye Study linking current smoking to exudative macular degeneration (PMID: 8452123), and reviews stating cigarette smoking is among the most consistent AMD risk factors (PMID: 19027484). Additional evidence supports smoking avoidance as a preventive strategy, including a modeling study predicting fewer AMD cases and blindness after smoking cessation (PMID: 18783631) and an older-women cohort where smoking increased early AMD risk in those aged 80+ (PMID: 19796757). Support for hypertension is present but less consistent: NHANES data associated systolic blood pressure and past hypertension with AMD prevalence (PMID: 3421236), an Argentine case-control study reported odds ratios of 2.1 for hypertension and 2.2 for smoking (PMID: 23828466), and a review listed untreated hypertension and smoking as basic risk factors (PMID: 21137473). Some supporting items are indirect or nonspecific to the AREDS cohort, and one review-style article gives broad assertions without detailed effect estimates (PMID: 21137473). Overall, the support side shows a recurring theme that smoking is a robust modifiable AMD risk factor, while hypertension has more limited and heterogeneous support.
contradict_summary: Two studies weaken the claim specifically for hypertension and, in one case, for smoking. A Brazilian cross-sectional study found no association of AMD with either hypertension (p=0.1985) or smoking (p=0.6374), directly conflicting with the claim (PMID: 15905946). An Iranian elderly cohort found smoking strongly associated with AMD but reported no association with hypertension, so this study contradicts only the hypertension component while actually supporting the smoking component despite being placed in the contradict group, indicating a categorization discrepancy (PMID: 26644880). The main contradictory theme is therefore inconsistency for hypertension rather than smoking. These contradictions also come from non-AREDS populations, limiting direct applicability to the specific claim context.
conclusion: The statement is overall more supported than contradicted. Evidence for smoking as an AMD risk factor is repeated across multiple studies and designs, whereas evidence for hypertension is mixed, with some supportive studies but notable null findings, so the smoking component is strong but the hypertension component is only moderately supported.

support_highlights.snippet: Among smokers who inhaled, the risk of atrophic maculopathy was 2.5 times higher than among non-smokers (P less than 0.01), whereas the use of hypnotics increased the risk of exudative maculopathy by 3.4 times (P less than 0.01).
BACKGROUND: Tobacco smoking is a risk factor for age-related macular degeneration, but studies of ex-smokers suggest quitting can reduce the risk.
RESULTS: If 1,000 smokers quit, our model predicted 48 fewer cases of macular degeneration, 12 fewer cases of blindness, and a gain of 1,600 QALYs.
CONCLUSION: Smoking cessation is unequivocally cost-effective in terms of its impact on age-related macular degeneration outcomes alone.
The most consistent risk factors associated with this ocular condition are increasing age and cigarette smoking.

There was an increased risk of early AMD among subjects aged 80 years or older who were smoking compared to those younger than 80 years who were not smoking (OR, 5.49; 95% CI, 1.57 to 19.20; P for interaction = .026).
CONCLUSIONS: The magnitude of the greater-than-additive effect of smoking on the age-adjusted risk of AMD reinforces recommendations to quit smoking even for older individuals.
The basic risk factors of the disease are smoking, untreated hypertension, atherosclerosis, cataract surgery, and appearance of the disease in the family.
Other factors included hypertension (OR: 2.1), smoking (OR: 2.2), and being of the Mestizo race, which lowered the risk of age-related macular degeneration (OR: 0.40).
PURPOSE: Smoking has been identified as a major modifiable risk factor for age-related macular degeneration (AMD).

Stratified analysis, adjusting for age, showed that education, systolic blood pressure, past history of hypertension, cerebrovascular disease, and refractive error were all associated with macular degeneration.
With the exception of education, these factors remained statistically significant when simultaneously entered into a logistic regression model.

The relative odds for exudative macular degeneration, one form of late age-related maculopathy, in females who were current smokers was 2.50 (95% confidence interval 1.01-6.20) compared with those who were ex-smokers or never smokers; for males, it was 3.29 (95% confidence interval 1.03-10.50).
These results suggest that exudative macular degeneration is associated with cigarette smoking and that different forms of macular degeneration may have different etiologies.

contradict_highlights.snippet: No age-related macular degeneration association with race (p=0.1367), hypertension (p=0.1985), diabetes (p=0.6712), smoking (p=0.6374), alcohol intake (p=0.7359), ocular sunlight exposure (p=0.9399), light-colored iris (p=0.1691), hyperopia (p=0.5098) and the usage of antioxidants (p=0.8251) was observed.
There was a significant association between AMD and smoking (P<0.001) but no association was seen with AMD and age, level of education, history of hyperlipidemia, hypertension and diabetes.
Multiple LRAs revealed that smoking increased AMD by odds ratio of 5.03 (95% confidence interval 2.47-10.23 p<0.001) as compared to nonsmokers.
CONCLUSION: According to our findings, the prevalence of AMD was relatively high and smoking increased the risk of AMD in the elderly population.

claim: In participants aged 60 to 80 years with age-related macular degeneration in the Age-Related Eye Disease Study, hyperopia, lens opacities, less education, female sex, increased body mass index, and white race were also associated with age-related macular degeneration.
support_summary: One supporting study found that lens opacifying disease was associated with increased odds of age-related macular degeneration, and this association persisted after adjustment for age, sex, and systolic blood pressure, supporting the lens opacity component of the claim (PMID: 2729473). Another study evaluating AMD risk factors reported that increased body mass index was considered a possible risk factor worth further study, but its abstract does not clearly establish significant associations for hyperopia, lens opacities, female sex, education, or white race despite listing them among evaluated variables (PMID: 9577116). Overall, the supporting evidence mainly aligns with lens opacities and, more weakly, higher BMI, rather than the full set of factors in the claim. There is also a scope discrepancy because these studies are not specifically the AREDS 60-80-year participant analysis described in the claim.
contradict_summary: A cross-sectional Brazilian study found positive associations between AMD and female gender, cataract, cataract surgery, and obesity, which is partly concordant with the claim, but it explicitly reported no association with race or hyperopia (PMID: 15905946). This directly weakens the claim's assertions regarding white race and hyperopia, while not addressing education and only indirectly relating cataract to lens opacity. Because the abstract contains both supportive and contradictory findings relative to the claim, this evidence is mixed but overall partially contradictory. The population and setting also differ from the AREDS cohort, limiting direct comparability.
conclusion: The statement is only partially supported overall and remains unclear based on the provided evidence. Support exists mainly for lens opacities and possibly higher BMI, but a contradictory study explicitly found no association with hyperopia or race, and none of the cited abstracts directly confirms the full AREDS-specific combination of hyperopia, less education, female sex, increased BMI, white race, and lens opacities.

support_highlights.snippet: Data from 3,087 persons age 45 or older in the National Health and Nutrition Survey, 1971-74, showed that subjects with lens opacifying disease had an increased odds for age-related macular degeneration (AMD) compared to those who had no lens opacities.
The association remained after controlling for age, sex, and systolic blood pressure (a common risk factor) in a logistic regression model.

The following risk factors were evaluated: age, sex, body mass index, history of general medical conditions, cigarette smoking, sun exposure, family history of AMD and ocular conditions such as iris color, lens opacities, hyperopia, gerontoxon and changes in retinal vessels.

CONCLUSIONS: The studies confirmed the role of age in the development of AMD and indicated cardiovascular disturbances, increased body mass index and light iris as the possible risk factors for AMD that are most worth further studying.

contradict_highlights.snippet: This association was also present in the female gender (p=0.0451), the presence of cataract (p=0.0447), cataract surgery (p=0.0432) and obesity (p<0.0001).

No age-related macular degeneration association with race (p=0.1367), hypertension (p=0.1985), diabetes (p=0.6712), smoking (p=0.6374), alcohol intake (p=0.7359), ocular sunlight exposure (p=0.9399), light-colored iris (p=0.1691), hyperopia (p=0.5098) and the usage of antioxidants (p=0.8251) was observed.
No age-related macular degeneration association with socioeconomic level was found in this study.

claim: In participants aged 60 to 80 years with age-related macular degeneration in the Age-Related Eye Disease Study, geographic atrophy was associated with thyroid hormone use and antacid use, and large drusen or extensive intermediate drusen were associated with arthritis and hydrochlorothiazide use.
support_summary: No non-zero supporting evidence items were available.
contradict_summary: No non-zero contradicting evidence items were available.
conclusion: This statement had only zero-score or missing-score rows in the input file.

claim: In patients with age-related macular degeneration, lutein supplementation increases macular pigment optical density compared with placebo.
support_summary: Multiple randomized placebo-controlled trials in AMD/ARMD patients report that lutein-containing supplementation increases macular pigment optical density (MPOD) versus placebo or no supplementation, including the Veterans LAST trial where MPOD rose by about 0.09 log units with lutein while placebo showed no significant change (PMID: 15117055) and LAST II showing MPOD increased with supplementation but declined slightly without it (PMID: 17478338). Several later RCTs in early AMD also found significant MPOD gains in active lutein groups, often with dose-response effects and no notable placebo change, including 20 mg lutein and lutein/zeaxanthin regimens over 48 weeks to 2 years (PMID: 22858124, PMID: 23312760, PMID: 25228440, PMID: 25815324). Additional supportive but less direct studies include lutein combined with DHA or zeaxanthin, where supplemented AMD groups had greater MPOD increases than placebo/control, though the independent effect of lutein alone cannot always be isolated (PMID: 23434908, PMID: 23519529, PMID: 22835510). Some SUPPORT items are only indirect or not placebo-controlled, such as observational/review/pilot studies or studies in healthy subjects rather than AMD patients, so they provide weaker relevance to the exact claim (PMID: 11157880, PMID: 12058502, PMID: 12672909, PMID: 17552428, PMID: 26950968). Overall, the dominant theme across the strongest evidence is that lutein-containing supplementation, especially at higher doses or among those with lower baseline MPOD, increases MPOD in AMD patients compared with placebo/no supplementation.
contradict_summary: Two randomized studies reported no statistically significant MPOD increase despite carotenoid exposure. One trial in healthy volunteers consuming lutein- or zeaxanthin-enriched eggs or a lutein-enriched beverage found substantial serum carotenoid increases but no macular pigment density change; however, this does not match the claim population because participants were healthy volunteers, not AMD patients (PMID: 24675775). A second randomized double-masked multicenter study including patients with neovascular AMD in one eye found no statistically significant MPOD increase in supplemented groups, but the study mixed retinal-pathology and non-pathology groups and used Visucam-based MPOD measurement, which the authors suggest may limit detection (PMID: 28336284). Thus, the contradicting evidence is limited in quantity and somewhat heterogeneous, with one study only partially applicable to AMD.
conclusion: The statement is overall more supported than contradicted. The strongest and most directly relevant evidence consists of several randomized placebo-controlled AMD trials consistently showing lutein-containing supplementation increases MPOD, whereas the contradictory evidence is sparse, includes one non-AMD population, and one mixed-population study with null findings.

claim: In patients with age-related macular degeneration, lutein supplementation does not produce a clear improvement in visual acuity compared with placebo.
support_summary: A randomized, double-blind, placebo-controlled trial in early AMD found that although lutein/zeaxanthin increased serum levels, macular pigment, and some contrast sensitivity measures, there were no statistical changes in best-spectacle corrected visual acuity during the trial, supporting lack of clear acuity benefit versus placebo (PMID: 25815324). A review article states that a definite association between lutein/zeaxanthin supplementation and clinical benefit has not yet been shown, despite limited data suggesting possible visual-function improvement, which indirectly supports the claim that visual acuity benefit is not clearly established (PMID: 18574012). Overall, the supporting theme is that biologic and some functional effects are seen, but clear improvement in visual acuity specifically is not demonstrated.
contradict_summary: The strongest contradictory evidence is the Veterans LAST randomized placebo-controlled trial, which reported that Snellen equivalent visual acuity improved by 5.4 letters with lutein alone and 3.5 letters with lutein plus antioxidants, while the placebo group had no significant changes, directly arguing against the claim (PMID: 15117055). Another randomized placebo-controlled trial in early AMD found only a trend toward better BCVA at 48 weeks, not a clear statistically significant acuity improvement, though contrast sensitivity improved significantly; this partially contradicts the claim mainly on broader visual-function outcomes rather than definitive acuity benefit (PMID: 22858124). There is also a discrepancy across studies, because PMID: 22858124 and PMID: 25815324 appear closely related in design/population yet differ in emphasis, with one reporting a BCVA trend and the other stating no statistical BCVA change.
conclusion: The evidence is mixed but leans slightly toward the claim being supported: larger/placebo-controlled evidence and review-level evidence suggest no clear visual acuity improvement, even when other visual measures improve. However, one placebo-controlled trial reported measurable acuity gains versus placebo, so the claim is not definitively settled.

support_highlights.snippet: No statistical changes of BCVA or FRT were observed during the trial.
Limited data have suggested that supplementation may also improve visual function.

A definite association between lutein and zeaxanthin supplementation and clinical benefit has yet to be shown; however, it may still be an appropriate cautionary measure for patients at high risk for developing AMD.

contradict_highlights.snippet: In Groups 1 L and 2 L/A, mean eye macular pigment optical density increased approximately 0.09 log units from baseline, Snellen equivalent visual acuity improved 5.4 letters for Group 1 L and 3.5 letters for Group 2 L/A, and contrast sensitivity improved.
Patients who received the placebo (Group 3) had no significant changes in any of the measured findings.
In this study, visual function is improved with lutein alone or lutein together with other nutrients.
At 48 weeks, a trend toward improvement was seen in BCVA, and there was a significant between-group difference in CS at 3 and 6 cycles/degree between the 20-mg lutein group and the placebo group.
Among patients with early AMD, supplementation with lutein and zeaxanthin improved macular pigment, which played a causative role in boosting visual function and might prevent the progression of AMD.

claim: In patients with age-related macular degeneration, lutein supplementation does not produce a clear improvement in macular function measured by microperimetry compared with placebo, although greater increases in macular pigment optical density are associated with greater improvements in visual function.
support_summary: Supportive evidence is limited and indirect. A review notes that lutein/zeaxanthin supplementation increases plasma levels and is positively associated with macular pigment optical density (MPOD), but clinical benefit remains unproven and visual-function data are limited, which is broadly consistent with the claim's cautious interpretation (PMID: 18574012). Another randomized study in subjects including neovascular AMD found no statistically significant increase in MPOD with carotenoid supplementation, though it did not assess microperimetry or placebo effects on macular function in the same way as the claim (PMID: 28336284). Overall, these studies support uncertainty about clear functional benefit more than they directly confirm the specific microperimetry-based statement. There is also a discrepancy because neither study directly addresses the claimed association between greater MPOD increase and greater visual-function improvement in AMD measured against placebo.
contradict_summary: Several randomized or prospective studies report that lutein-containing supplementation improves visual outcomes in AMD or related early macular disease, often alongside increased MPOD, which argues against a blanket statement of no clear functional improvement. Placebo-controlled trials found significant gains in contrast sensitivity, mfERG response densities, or visual acuity with lutein or lutein/zeaxanthin, and some reported positive correlations between MPOD increases and improved visual measures (PMID: 25815324, PMID: 15117055, PMID: 22835510, PMID: 22858124, PMID: 27064326). Additional non-placebo or mixed-formulation studies also reported improved retinal sensitivity or contrast sensitivity, though they are less directly comparable to the claim because they did not use microperimetry-vs-placebo specifically or included DHA/meso-zeaxanthin combinations (PMID: 21850440, PMID: 23434908, PMID: 24887490). A recurring theme is that benefits are more evident for some functional endpoints than others, with some studies showing no change in BCVA or dark adaptation despite improved MPOD or contrast sensitivity (PMID: 25815324, PMID: 27064326). Thus, contradiction is substantial but partly weakened by endpoint differences from the claim's specific focus on microperimetry.
conclusion: The statement is overall more contradicted than supported by the cited literature. Although a small amount of indirect evidence supports uncertainty about clear benefit, multiple controlled studies in AMD report improvements in visual function and/or positive associations between MPOD increases and functional gains after lutein-containing supplementation, even if they do not all use microperimetry specifically.

support_highlights.snippet: Increased dietary intake of or supplementation with lutein and zeaxanthin was found to result in increased plasma levels, which were positively and significantly associated with macular pigment optical density.
Limited data have suggested that supplementation may also improve visual function.

A definite association between lutein and zeaxanthin supplementation and clinical benefit has yet to be shown; however, it may still be an appropriate cautionary measure for patients at high risk for developing AMD.
No statistically significant increase in MPOD was noted in either group, even after discontinuation of the supplementation.
CONCLUSION: No significant improvement in MPOD seems to be detected with the Visucam

contradict_highlights.snippet: Serum lutein concentration and MPOD significantly increased in all the active treatment groups.
Supplementation with 20 mg lutein was the most effective in increasing MPOD and CS at 3 cycles/degree for the first 48 weeks.
CONCLUSIONS: Long-term lutein supplementation could increase serum lutein concentration, MPOD, and visual sensitivities of early AMD patients.
MPOD increased significantly from 0.45 to 0.52 and BCVA improved significantly from -0.04 to -0.09 LogMar.

Conclusion. Daily consumption of a dairy drink containing lutein-enriched egg yolks for one year improves visual acuity, MPOD, and plasma lutein concentration in elderly subjects with drusen and/or retinal pigment epithelial abnormalities.

In Groups 1 L and 2 L/A, mean eye macular pigment optical density increased approximately 0.09 log units from baseline, Snellen equivalent visual acuity improved 5.4 letters for Group 1 L and 3.5 letters for Group 2 L/A, and contrast sensitivity improved.
Patients who received the placebo (Group 3) had no significant changes in any of the measured findings.
CONCLUSION: In this study, visual function is improved with lutein alone or lutein together with other nutrients.
We observed significant increases in the contrast sensitivity at 1 year (p = 0.0124) and in the retinal sensitivity at 6 months (p < 0.0001) and 1 year (p < 0.0001).
However, supplementation seems to improve visual functions such as contrast sensitivity and retinal sensitivity.

After 48-week supplementation, the N1P1 response densities showed significant increases in ring 1 for the 20 mg lutein group and for the lutein and zeaxanthin group, and in ring 2 for the 20 mg lutein group.
The increases in MPOD related positively to the increases in N1P1 response density in ring 1 and ring 2 for nearly all active treatment groups.
CONCLUSION: Early functional abnormalities of the central retina in the early AMD patients could be improved by lutein and zeaxanthin supplementation.
These improvements may be potentially attributed to the elevations in MPOD.

Macular pigment optical density increased significantly by a mean ± standard error of 0.076 ± 0.022 density unit in the 20-mg lutein group and 0.058 ± 0.027 density unit in the lutein and zeaxanthin group during 48 weeks.
At 48 weeks, a trend toward improvement was seen in BCVA, and there was a significant between-group difference in CS at 3 and 6 cycles/degree between the 20-mg lutein group and the placebo group.

The increase in MPOD related positively to the reduction in the logarithm of the minimum angle of resolution BCVA (r = -0.31; P<0.01) and the increases in CS at 4 spatial frequencies (r ranging from 0.26 to 0.38; all P<0.05).

CONCLUSIONS: Among patients with early AMD, supplementation with lutein and zeaxanthin improved macular pigment, which played a causative role in boosting visual function and might prevent the progression of AMD.
One year later, the mean MPOD had increased by 0.059 in the placebo group and by 0.162 in patients receiving lutein and DHA.
This difference between groups was significant (p < 0.05).
Lutein and DHA supplementation is effective in increasing the MPOD and may aid in prevention of age related macular degeneration.

A statistically significant increase in the macular pigment optical density was observed at all measured eccentricities in Group 2 (P ≤ 0.005) and in Group 3 (P < 0.05, for all), but only at 1.75° in Group 1 (P = 0.018).

Statistically significant (P < 0.05) improvements in letter contrast sensitivity were seen at all spatial frequencies (except 1.2 cycles per degree) in Group 3, and at low spatial frequencies in Groups 1 and 2.

CONCLUSION: Augmentation of the macular pigment optical density across its spatial profile and enhancements in contrast sensitivity were best achieved after supplementation with a formulation containing high doses of meso-zeaxanthin in combination with lutein and zeaxanthin.

claim: In patients with neovascular age-related macular degeneration, ranibizumab and bevacizumab produced similar improvements in visual acuity over a 2-year period.
support_summary: Several comparative studies report broadly similar visual acuity outcomes with bevacizumab and ranibizumab in neovascular AMD, including a 2-year comparison showing gains of 7.0 vs 9.2 letters with no significant difference (PMID: 27162727). Real-world registry data also found that the 2-year risk of poor vision did not depend on whether patients received ranibizumab or bevacizumab (PMID: 28141748). Additional shorter-term or indirect studies found similar visual acuity gains or no apparent differences between drugs, but many did not specifically assess a full 2-year head-to-head comparison (PMID: 19384786, PMID: 19590252, PMID: 19696701, PMID: 30810619). Some SUPPORT items are only indirect support because they address 1-year outcomes, mixed anti-VEGF cohorts without separate drug comparisons, or review-level statements rather than direct 2-year randomized evidence (PMID: 22211145, PMID: 24637667).
contradict_summary: Contradicting studies mainly come from switch analyses or subgroup analyses rather than direct general-population 2-year head-to-head comparisons. In a switch study, visual acuity decreased after changing from ranibizumab to bevacizumab, suggesting a possible small disadvantage for bevacizumab, although disease progression could also explain the change (PMID: 25196907). A retrospective subgroup study in patients with incomplete posterior vitreous detachment found greater visual and anatomic improvement with ranibizumab than bevacizumab over 12 months, but this applies to a specific subgroup and not necessarily all neovascular AMD patients (PMID: 26692256). One CATT-related cohort found bevacizumab associated with a higher risk of sustained visual acuity loss over 2 years, while another cited item is actually more supportive than contradictory because it states monthly bevacizumab was noninferior to monthly ranibizumab at 1 year, indicating a categorization discrepancy (PMID: 24875610, PMID: 21961847). Another switching study suggested ranibizumab-only or bevacizumab-to-ranibizumab groups performed better than ranibizumab-to-bevacizumab, but this was retrospective and confounded by switching context (PMID: 23516253).
conclusion: The claim is overall more supported than contradicted. The strongest directly relevant evidence includes 2-year comparative data and real-world 2-year outcomes showing no significant visual acuity difference between ranibizumab and bevacizumab, whereas much of the contradictory evidence comes from subgroup or treatment-switch settings that are less directly applicable to the general claim.

support_highlights.snippet: RESULTS: Visual acuity at 2y bevacizumab patients gained 7.0 letters and ranibizumab 9.2 (P=0.31, 95% CI -6.4 to 2.0).
The risk of having a visual acuity lower than 60 letters does not depend on the choice of treatment drug.

RESULTS: The treatment with Avastin is completed in 319 eyes with an average improvement of the visual acuity of 5.1 letters after 3.3 injections, and with Lucentis in 226 eyes with an average improvement of the visual acuity of 6.4 letters after 3.4 injections (p = 0.24; one way ANOVA).
CONCLUSION: Both of the drugs allow the drying of the macular findings in the great majority of the cases after a short time and lead to a quite similar improvement of the visual acuity.
The mean logMAR equivalent of BCVA at 1 month after the injection improved by 0.18 in the bevacizumab group (p = 0.009) and by 0.13 in the ranibizumab group (p = 0.004).
CONCLUSIONS: Bevacizumab and ranibizumab treatments resulted in similar gains in visual acuity and reduction in macular thickness, documented each month following injection.
Best-obtained visual acuity during treatment was 20/63 with bevacizumab and 20/63 with ranibizumab (P = 0.5).
Mean visual acuity at the last ranibizumab follow-up visit was 20/80 (P = 0.49).
CONCLUSION: In this subset of patients with neovascular AMD switched from bevacizumab to ranibizumab therapy, there were no apparent differences in visual acuity outcomes or injection rates.
Many studies have indicated that Ranibizumab and Bevacizumab are of roughly equal short-term efficacy and safety, Bevacizumab is an attractive alternative to Ranibizumab due to its lower cost.
After 1 year of follow-up, we found statistically significant improvements in visual acuity and central macular thickness reduction in all groups.
However, we found no statistically significant differences between the three groups.
CONCLUSIONS: The bi-weekly follow-up was effective and we found no significant differences in efficacy or safety between the treatments with ranibizumab and bevacizumab.

contradict_highlights.snippet: After the switch, there was a statistically significant decrease in BCVA to 51.7 letters (p < 0.001).

CONCLUSION: Switching patients to bevacizumab may have a minor negative effect on the initial gain obtained with ranibizumab; however the degenerative history of wet AMD could explain this small variation in visual acuity.

In subjects with vitreous attachment to the fovea, the ranibizumab cohort had a greater improvement in visual acuity (0.18 ± 0.1 vs 0.04 ± 0.11 logMAR) (P = 0.0176) and a greater reduction in macular thickness (93.4 ± 32.2 μm vs 30.3 ± 28.3 μm) (P = 0.0064) compared with the bevacizumab cohort.
CONCLUSIONS: Neovascular AMD patients with vitreous attachment to the fovea may have better visual and anatomic outcomes when treated with ranibizumab compared with bevacizumab.
Visual acuity results for bevacizumab as needed were inconclusive for noninferiority relative to monthly administration of either drug.
After switching, BCVA continuously improved in the bevacizumab to ranibizumab group, but stayed stable in the ranibizumab to bevacizumab group.
The BCVA worsened in 20% of eyes in the bevacizumab to ranibizumab group and in 40% of eyes in the ranibizumab to bevacizumab group.
CONCLUSION: The ranibizumab-only group and the switching from bevacizumab to ranibizumab group seemed superior to the ranibizumab to bevacizumab group.

Baseline factors independently associated with a higher incidence of sustained visual acuity loss were the presence of nonfoveal GA (odds ratio [OR],  2.86; 95% CI, 1.35-6.08; P = .006), larger area of choroidal neovascularization (OR for a >4-disc area vs ≤1-disc area, 3.91; 95% CI, 1.70-9.03; P = .007), and bevacizumab treatment (OR,  1.83; 95% CI, 1.07-3.14; P = .03).
Risk was 3% higher among eyes treated with bevacizumab.

claim: In patients with neovascular age-related macular degeneration, as-needed dosing produced less improvement in visual acuity than monthly dosing, whether started at treatment initiation or after switching from monthly treatment.
support_summary: Several studies support that less frequent or PRN dosing yields smaller visual acuity gains than regular dosing in neovascular AMD. A retrospective pooled analysis of phase III/IIIb ranibizumab trials found that after 3 monthly loading doses, continued monthly dosing produced more delayed ≥15-letter responders and better maintenance of gains than switching to less frequent regimens, directly matching the claim's post-switch component (PMID: 23251787). Other studies reported that PRN from baseline or after loading was associated with smaller gains or lower responder rates than more regular schedules, including loading-dose versus PRN comparisons (PMID: 20693484), PRN from treatment initiation not achieving the efficacy of regular monthly injections (PMID: 20890885), and PRN performing worse than bimonthly aflibercept (PMID: 28402983). Additional indirect or lower-specificity support comes from clinician-determined retreatment appearing less effective than trial regimens (PMID: 20937739), modeling suggesting better vision-related outcomes with monthly than PRN ranibizumab (PMID: 24979237), routine-practice data favoring treat-and-extend over PRN though this is not a monthly comparison (PMID: 38976148), and regular anti-VEGF treatment outperforming scarce treatment in poor-vision nAMD (PMID: 39167570); these latter studies are supportive but not exact matches to the claim.
contradict_summary: Contradicting evidence is mixed and includes some studies that may be better viewed as neutral rather than truly contradictory. A randomized trial found ranibizumab treat-and-extend was not worse than monthly dosing over 24 months, but this addresses T&E rather than PRN/as-needed dosing, so it is a discrepancy in categorization relative to the claim (PMID: 31917441). A retrospective comparison of monthly versus variable dosing after 3 loading injections found similar visual acuity outcomes at 12 months, with only a nonsignificant trend favoring monthly treatment, which weakens but does not clearly refute the claim (PMID: 21926941). One PRN ranibizumab case series showed visual acuity improvement/stabilization with PRN treatment from baseline but did not directly compare against monthly dosing, so it is only indirect contradiction at most (PMID: 21400056). An expert commentary on CATT stated that PRN bevacizumab visual acuity results were inconclusive for noninferiority versus monthly dosing and suggested modified monthly regimens are feasible, which is not a direct contradiction and is partly consistent with possible inferiority of PRN (PMID: 21961847).
conclusion: Overall, the statement is more supported than contradicted. The strongest and most claim-relevant evidence shows better visual acuity outcomes with continued monthly dosing than with switching to less frequent/PRN regimens and generally poorer gains with PRN strategies, whereas the opposing studies are mostly indirect, neutral, or address treat-and-extend rather than true as-needed dosing.

support_highlights.snippet: After three monthly loading doses, continued monthly dosing resulted in further gains, as there were more delayed 15-letter responders at month 12 (14.7-16.1%) than with less frequent dosing (5.0-6.0%).
Monthly dosing also resulted in more patients maintaining VA gains at later time points.
Continued monthly dosing resulted in a greater percentage of patients gaining ≥15 letters than with switching to less frequent dosing regimens.

At 24 months, patients on the T&E regimen experienced significantly greater mean (95% confidence interval) improvement in VA than those on PRN (+ 4.2 [3.1, 5.2] vs. + 1.3 [0.1, 2.6] letters; p < 0.001), with more injections (14.9 standard deviation(SD) 4.3) vs. 9.8(SD 4.3); p < 0.001).
Eyes treated with a T&E regimen had better VA outcomes from VEGF inhibitors than eyes treated PRN.
However, twice as many patients improved visual acuity by 15 or more letters in the LD group (29.8% in the LD group compared with 12.9% in the PRN group (p=0.01)).
This study showed that standard protocols used for OCT-guided retreatment achieved smaller mean gains in vision than those obtained with monthly ranibizumab administration.
Further, loading doses of ranibizumab resulted in more visual gains than the PRN protocol.
Mean visual acuity change was an increase of 1.3 letters (95% confidence interval -2.7 to +5.3), and difference between angiographic patterns did not reach statistical significance (p=0.30).

As-needed treatment from the start achieved stabilization of visual acuity and a moderate decrease of retinal thickness with a low number of injections, but did not achieve the same efficacy as regular monthly injections.
CONCLUSIONS: Clinician-determined re-treatment after a three-dose initiation phase appears to be less effective in improving BCVA than in randomised controlled trials.
Monthly 0.5 mg intravitreal ranibizumab would reduce incident blindness by 72% (95% simulation interval, 70-74%). Ranibizumab given as needed would reduce incident blindness by 68% (64-71%).

Without treatment, 4846 individuals would become visually impaired over 2 years; this proportion would be reduced by 37% (34-39%) with monthly intravitreal ranibizumab, and by 28% (23-33%) with ranibizumab given as needed.
BCVA was significantly improved only in the bimonthly group at 12 months.
BCVA showed significant improvement only in the bimonthly group but not in the PRN group at 12 months.

Improvements in visual acuity, reduction in central subfield macular thickness, and maximal height of choroidal neovascularization were significantly favorable in the regular treatment group at Year 1, and central subfield macular thickness was significantly decreased at Year 2.
Survival analysis revealed that the regular treatment group had significantly greater preservation of visual acuity and lesion size than that in the scarce treatment group.

Maintaining intravitreal anti-vascular endothelial growth factor treatment for patients with neovascular age-related macular degeneration and poor vision showed significant advantages in visual acuity and lesion size stability and reduced the incidence of new subretinal hemorrhage, which suggests preservation of paracentral vision.

contradict_highlights.snippet: At 12 months, the mean logarithm of the minimum angle of resolution (logMAR) visual acuity improved by 0.078 logMAR units (P = 0.046) and the mean central retinal thickness decreased by 85.7 μm (P < 0.001).
Thirty of 41 eyes (73.2%) avoided any loss of vision, and 20 eyes (48.8 %) showed improved visual acuity.
Ranibizumab administered on an as-needed basis may stabilize visual acuity in patients with neovascular AMD.
The mean (SD) BCVA improvement was not worse with the T&E arm, 6.8 (14.1) letters vs 6.0 (12.6) letters, compared with the monthly arm (difference, 0.9; 95% CI, -1.6 to 3.3; P = .21).

There was a gain of 15 or more letters in 25.5% of the T&E group and 23.1% of the monthly treatment group (difference, 2.4%; 95% CI, -6.8% to 11.6%; P = .59) and a loss of 15 or more letters in 6.5% of the T&E group and 5.8% of the monthly treatment group (difference, -0.7%; 95% CI, -9.9% to 8.5%; P = .85).

These findings suggest that change in vision from baseline is not worse with a T&E compared with a monthly regimen of ranibizumab for nAMD through 24 months, achieving clinically meaningful improvements in BCVA despite fewer injections and visits.

At 12 months the median number of injections were 12 and 8, respectively, and the mean change in Snellen visual acuity was an improvement of 0.27 logarithm of the minimum angle of resolution in the monthly treated group versus 0.21 logarithm of the minimum angle of resolution improvement in the variable-dosing group (P = 0.53).
In the monthly treated group 96.8% of eyes lost <0.3 logarithm of the minimum angle of resolution versus 96.6% of eyes in the variable-dosing group (P = 1.0).
We were able to show that in our clinical setting patients achieved similar visual acuity results with either monthly injections or with a variable-dosing protocol.
Visual acuity results for bevacizumab as needed were inconclusive for noninferiority relative to monthly administration of either drug.
The trial supports use of either drug as primary therapy and suggests that modification of monthly dosing regimens is feasible.

claim: In patients with neovascular age-related macular degeneration, ranibizumab and bevacizumab had similar rates of death and arteriothrombotic events, but bevacizumab was associated with more systemic serious adverse events.
support_summary: The strongest direct support comes from the CATT randomized noninferiority trial, which reported similar rates of death, myocardial infarction, and stroke between bevacizumab and ranibizumab, but a higher proportion of serious systemic adverse events with bevacizumab (24.1% vs 19.0%) in neovascular AMD (PMID: 21526923). A review/commentary on CATT also notes that bevacizumab had a higher reported rate of serious adverse events, although it does not add direct data on death or arteriothrombotic events and emphasizes that safety findings need further analysis (PMID: 21970950). One retrospective bevacizumab-versus-control study found more hospital admissions but no increase in arteriothrombotic admissions with bevacizumab; however, it did not compare bevacizumab with ranibizumab, so it only indirectly supports the SAE portion of the claim and not the head-to-head comparison (PMID: 23569355). Overall, support is driven mainly by one large randomized trial, while the other supporting items are weaker or only partially relevant.
contradict_summary: Several head-to-head studies reported no significant difference in serious systemic adverse events between bevacizumab and ranibizumab, including IVAN (PMID: 22578446), GEFAL (PMID: 23916488), LUCAS 2-year results (PMID: 26477842), an Austrian randomized trial (PMID: 23292928), and a retrospective cohort analysis (PMID: 23255862). Some studies also suggested fewer arteriothrombotic events with bevacizumab rather than similar rates, notably IVAN and LUCAS, which conflicts with the claim’s statement of similar event rates (PMID: 22578446; PMID: 25227499). A large population-based nested case-control study found no significant differences between the drugs for ischemic stroke, myocardial infarction, venous thromboembolism, or heart failure, contradicting the assertion that bevacizumab causes more systemic serious adverse events (PMID: 22763393). Several smaller retrospective studies reported no serious complications or no systemic adverse events, but many of these are limited by size and often focus on ocular rather than systemic safety, so they are weaker contradictions or only partially relevant (PMID: 23040443; PMID: 23638418; PMID: 24558597). One listed contradicting commentary is actually mixed, because it acknowledges higher observed serious adverse events with bevacizumab while noting similar cardiac and neurologic event counts, reflecting discrepancy within the evidence set rather than clear contradiction (PMID: 21961847).
conclusion: The statement is overall mixed but somewhat contradicted by the broader evidence base. Although the large CATT trial strongly supports the claim, multiple other comparative trials and observational studies generally found no significant excess of systemic serious adverse events with bevacizumab and in some cases reported fewer arteriothrombotic events, so the totality of evidence does not consistently uphold the claim.

support_highlights.snippet: Rates of death, myocardial infarction, and stroke were similar for patients receiving either bevacizumab or ranibizumab (P>0.20).

The proportion of patients with serious systemic adverse events (primarily hospitalizations) was higher with bevacizumab than with ranibizumab (24.1% vs. 19.0%; risk ratio, 1.29; 95% confidence interval, 1.01 to 1.66), with excess events broadly distributed in disease categories not identified in previous studies as areas of concern.

While CATT provided further evidence for the efficacy of bevacizumab treatment, differences in adverse events between the two treatments (e.g. a higher rate of serious adverse events with bevacizumab compared with ranibizumab) were reported; however, these still have to be analysed, with the larger sample sizes of previous ranibizumab studies needing to be taken into account.
There were significantly more hospital admissions among bevacizumab-treated patients than in the control group (P = 0.039).
Sub-analysis of hospitalizations due to arteriothrombotic causes did not reveal a statistically significant difference between groups (P = 0.629).

contradict_highlights.snippet: No death or thromboembolic events were observed.
Intraocular bevacizumab appears to be well tolerated for the treatment of a variety of retinal and choroidal vascular diseases.
Fewer participants receiving bevacizumab had an arteriothrombotic event or heart failure (odds ratio [OR], 0.23; 95% CI, 0.05 to 1.07; P = 0.03).
There was no difference between drugs in the proportion experiencing a serious systemic adverse event (OR, 1.35; 95% CI, 0.80 to 2.27; P = 0.25).
Other outcomes are consistent with the drugs and treatment regimens having similar efficacy and safety.

Similarly, a secondary analysis of exclusive users of bevacizumab or ranibizumab showed no differences in risk between the two drugs (adjusted odds ratios for bevacizumab relative to ranibizumab of 1.03 (0.67 to 1.60) for ischaemic stroke, 1.23 (0.85 to 1.77) for acute myocardial infarction, 0.92 (0.51 to 1.69) for venous thromboembolism, and 1.35 (0.93 to 1.95) for congestive heart failure).

CONCLUSIONS: Intravitreal injections of bevacizumab and ranibizumab were not associated with significant risks of ischaemic stroke, acute myocardial infarction, congestive heart failure, or venous thromboembolism.
No serious ocular adverse events were noted in both groups.
Systemic adverse events occurred in 10 of 702 patients (1.4%): 7 in the bevacizumab group (7/503; 1.4%) and 3 in the ranibizumab group (3/199; 1.5%).
This difference was not statistically significant (Fisher's exact test, P = 0.573).
In conclusion, compared to ranibizumab, bevacizumab may not increase the risk of systemic adverse events in patients receiving intravitreal injections.
There were no severe local adverse reactions or systemic adverse events.
CONCLUSION: Intravitreal bevacizumab and ranibizumab have equivalent effects on BCVA and CRT and appeare safe over the short-term.
The proportion of patients with serious adverse events was 12.6% in the bevacizumab group and 12.1% in the ranibizumab group (P = 0.88).
The proportion of patients with serious systemic or ocular adverse events was similar in both groups.
CONCLUSIONS: Bevacizumab was noninferior to ranibizumab for visual acuity at 1 year with similar safety profiles.
The number of serious adverse events was similar between the groups over the course of the study.
There was no significant difference in the number of serious adverse events between the treatment groups.

Bevacizumab groups had a statistically higher observed risk of serious adverse events; however, scrutiny of the online supplements shows similar numbers of cardiac and neurologic events in bevacizumab and ranibizumab users.
A difference in cardiovascular safety between the 2 drugs was not apparent on inspection of the supplementary safety data.
Furthermore, there were no significant differences in the decrease of retinal thickness, change of lesion size and number of adverse events between the groups.
CONCLUSIONS: Bevacizumab was equivalent to ranibizumab for visual acuity at all time points over 1 year.
There was no significant difference of decrease of retinal thickness or number of adverse events.
No serious complications were detected in any of the groups.

Conclusion. Both of the bevacizumab and ranibizumab found to be effective in the treatment of nAMD in regards of functional and anatomical outcomes with similar number of treatments and similar side effects.
There were fewer arteriothrombotic events in the bevacizumab group (1.4%) than in the ranibizumab group (4.5%) (P = 0.050) and significantly more cardiac events in the ranibizumab group (P = 0.036).
The numbers of serious adverse events were small.

claim: In participants aged 60 to 80 years with age-related macular degeneration in the Age-Related Eye Disease Study, smoking and hypertension were associated with a higher likelihood of age-related macular degeneration, supporting smoking avoidance and hypertension prevention as potential strategies to reduce risk.
support_summary: Supporting studies consistently link cigarette smoking with higher risk, incidence, or progression of AMD, including several AREDS analyses showing smoking associated with neovascular AMD or progression to advanced AMD (PMID: 15808240, 24385141, 16816528, 21139980). A subset of supporting studies also report positive associations between hypertension or higher blood pressure and AMD outcomes, including neovascular AMD, exudative AMD, or incident ARM/AMD (PMID: 17953990, 9893587, 12939290, 24460653, 18222193, 26950426, 31595025, 3947296, 9194725). Several abstracts suggest dose-response patterns for smoking and note lower risk after smoking cessation, reinforcing the prevention implication for smoking avoidance (PMID: 8859077, 16361672, 8953988, 8827966). However, much of the support is indirect to the exact claim because many studies are outside AREDS, focus on progression/late AMD rather than overall AMD likelihood, or support smoking but not hypertension. Overall, the support side shows a strong recurring theme for smoking and a moderate but less uniform theme for hypertension/blood pressure.
contradict_summary: Contradicting evidence includes multiple studies reporting no significant association of smoking and/or hypertension with AMD or ARM, especially for hypertension: for example no predictive effect of smoking or hypertension on fellow-eye progression in SST (PMID: 19734762), no association with hypertension or smoking in a Brazilian cross-sectional study (PMID: 15905946), and no association of blood pressure/hypertension with AMD in SHIP-Trend (PMID: 31446436). Several population-based studies specifically found hypertension or blood pressure not associated with incident or prevalent AMD/ARM (PMID: 11471092, 8643241, 8460013, 17275090), while some others found smoking associated but hypertension not associated, creating only partial contradiction to the combined claim (PMID: 20547953, 9596493, 26644880). One item appears only weakly relevant because it concerns small hard drusen rather than AMD itself, so its contradiction is indirect (PMID: 36548342). Overall, the contradiction is driven mainly by inconsistent evidence for hypertension, whereas contradiction of smoking is less consistent because several “contradicting” studies still support smoking as a risk factor.
conclusion: The statement is overall more supported than contradicted. Evidence for smoking as a higher-risk factor for AMD is strong and recurrent, including within AREDS-related analyses, while evidence for hypertension is mixed but supported by several studies; the main uncertainty is that hypertension associations are less consistent across cohorts than smoking.

claim: In participants aged 60 to 80 years with age-related macular degeneration in the Age-Related Eye Disease Study, hyperopia, lens opacities, less education, female sex, increased body mass index, and white race were also associated with age-related macular degeneration.
support_summary: Several studies support parts of the claim, especially associations of AMD with white race, hyperopia, lens opacity/cataract, lower education, and higher BMI. Within AREDS specifically, a prospective analysis found white race associated with incident neovascular AMD, and less education plus obesity associated with central geographic atrophy, but it did not identify hyperopia, lens opacities, or female sex as significant factors in the abstract (PMID: 15808240). Outside AREDS, case-control and population studies reported hyperopia and lens opacities/cataract as AMD risk factors (PMID: 9893587, PMID: 6881127, PMID: 7125436), hyperopia and lower education as associated with age-related maculopathy (PMID: 16774957), and higher BMI/obesity as associated with AMD incidence or progression in some cohorts (PMID: 12796248, PMID: 24667857). Multiple studies also consistently found higher AMD prevalence in white versus black populations (PMID: 10366070, PMID: 18268216, PMID: 11190014, PMID: 687553). A discrepancy is that many supporting studies only address subsets of the claimed factors or different populations/settings rather than reproducing the full AREDS finding.
contradict_summary: Contradictory evidence is limited and mostly challenges specific components rather than the whole claim. One Indian population-based study found no direct association between AMD presence/severity and obesity indices, arguing against the BMI component, though in a different population from AREDS (PMID: 29941743). A Brazilian cross-sectional study found no association with hyperopia or race, but did observe associations with female sex, cataract, and obesity, making it mixed rather than wholly contradictory (PMID: 15905946). Another study linked small hard drusen to lower BMI and hyperopia, but explicitly stated these drusen are not an early manifestation of AMD, so its relevance to contradiction of AMD risk factors is indirect and somewhat miscategorized (PMID: 36548342).
conclusion: The statement is overall more supported than contradicted. Support is strongest for white race, higher BMI, lower education, and hyperopia/lens opacity associations across AREDS-related or broader AMD studies, while contradiction is sparse, population-specific, and mainly limited to the BMI or hyperopia components rather than the full claim.

claim: In participants aged 60 to 80 years with age-related macular degeneration in the Age-Related Eye Disease Study, geographic atrophy was associated with thyroid hormone use and antacid use, and large drusen or extensive intermediate drusen were associated with arthritis and hydrochlorothiazide use.
support_summary: No supporting evidence items were provided.
contradict_summary: A large matched case-control study found that, after adjustment, thyroid hormone use and thiazide diuretics were not associated with AMD overall, while antacid use showed only a small residual association that the authors suggested might reflect confounding rather than a true drug effect (PMID: 17544775). Another AREDS analysis examined geographic atrophy enlargement rate rather than baseline presence of GA or drusen phenotypes; although thyroid hormone use was associated in univariable analysis, only cholesterol-lowering medication use and diuretic use remained independently associated in multivariable models, so this does not directly support the specific claim and is misclassified as purely contradicting evidence (PMID: 34489337). Neither contradicting study addresses the claim's reported associations of large drusen/extensive intermediate drusen with arthritis and hydrochlorothiazide use. Overall, the main contradictory theme is lack of robust adjusted evidence for thyroid hormone or thiazide associations with AMD, with some ambiguity for antacids and for GA progression versus GA presence.
conclusion: The statement is overall more contradicted than supported based on the provided evidence. There is no direct supporting evidence here, and the strongest available study argues against adjusted associations of thyroid hormone and thiazide use with AMD, while the second study addresses GA progression rather than the specific cross-sectional phenotype associations in the claim.

claim: In patients with age-related macular degeneration, lutein supplementation increases macular pigment optical density compared with placebo.
support_summary: Multiple randomized, double-masked/placebo-controlled trials in AMD consistently report increased macular pigment optical density (MPOD) with lutein-containing supplementation versus placebo or no supplementation, including the LAST trial in atrophic AMD (PMID: 15117055), CLEAR in early AMD (PMID: 23385792), and several 48-week to 2-year early-AMD trials showing significant MPOD gains and dose-response effects with 10–20 mg lutein (PMID: 22858124; PMID: 25228440; PMID: 23312760; PMID: 25815324). Some studies also found placebo groups remained stable or declined while treated groups increased, and benefits were often greater in participants with lower baseline MPOD (PMID: 17478338; PMID: 23695657). A 2022 systematic review/meta-analysis concluded lutein supplementation significantly increases MPOD in AMD, with larger effects at higher dose and longer duration (PMID: 35433928). Many additional supportive items are weaker because they used mixed carotenoid formulations, lacked placebo, or included mostly-but-not-exclusively AMD populations, so they support the direction of effect but are less specific to the exact claim (eg, PMID: 17306793; PMID: 23434908; PMID: 24103519). Overall, the strongest abstract-level evidence directly supports that lutein supplementation increases MPOD in AMD compared with placebo.
contradict_summary: A small number of studies reported no significant MPOD increase despite lutein-containing supplementation. In Japanese subjects including many fellow eyes of AMD patients, 6 mg/day lutein for 1 year produced no time-dependent MPOD change, although visual function improved, suggesting dose may have been insufficient (PMID: 21850440). Another randomized study using Visucam measurements found no significant MPOD increase after lutein/zeaxanthin supplementation in patients with neovascular AMD or post-cataract controls, raising the possibility of measurement-method limitations or population differences (PMID: 28336284). One additional negative RCT was in offspring of patients with neovascular AMD rather than AMD patients themselves, so it is only indirectly relevant to the claim despite showing no MPOD change versus placebo after 6 months (PMID: 28973076). Thus, contradictory evidence exists but is less directly aligned with the target population and may reflect lower dose, shorter duration, advanced phenotype, or measurement issues.
conclusion: The statement is overall more supported than contradicted. Several directly relevant placebo-controlled RCTs and a meta-analysis in AMD patients show significant lutein-associated increases in MPOD, whereas the contradictory studies are fewer and are partly limited by lower dose, different measurement methods, or populations not fully matching AMD patients.

claim: In patients with age-related macular degeneration, lutein supplementation does not produce a clear improvement in visual acuity compared with placebo.
support_summary: Several studies in AMD or age-related maculopathy report that lutein-containing supplementation increased serum lutein and/or macular pigment but did not significantly improve best-corrected visual acuity (BCVA) or visual acuity measures, including a 2-year randomized placebo-controlled trial in early AMD with no statistical BCVA change versus placebo despite gains in MPOD and contrast sensitivity (PMID: 25815324). Additional non-placebo or uncontrolled studies also found unchanged BCVA after lutein-based supplementation while other visual parameters such as contrast sensitivity or MPOD improved (PMID: 26720458, PMID: 25976647, PMID: 17122107, PMID: 27587715). A small randomized trial reported no changes between treated and non-treated groups during supplementation on retinal/visual function, consistent with no clear visual acuity benefit (PMID: 23084077). One cited trial is only indirectly relevant because it enrolled unaffected offspring of AMD patients rather than AMD patients and did not assess visual acuity outcomes (PMID: 28973076), and another focused on contrast sensitivity rather than acuity (PMID: 17268417). Overall, the recurring theme is biomarker or contrast-sensitivity improvement without a clear, reproducible visual-acuity benefit, though much of this evidence is limited by lack of placebo comparison or mixed formulations.
contradict_summary: Multiple randomized controlled trials directly contradict the claim by reporting better visual acuity outcomes in lutein-containing groups than placebo or no-treatment controls, including the Veterans LAST trial showing Snellen acuity gains with lutein while placebo showed no significant change (PMID: 15117055), the LUTEGA placebo-controlled trial showing significant BCVA improvement versus placebo after 12 months (PMID: 23695657), and a large placebo-controlled trial reporting an approximately 4.8-letter BCVA advantage at 36 months in the active group (PMID: 23218821). Other studies also reported significant VA/BCVA improvement or stabilization with lutein-containing interventions, though often as part of multi-nutrient formulations or foods rather than lutein alone (PMID: 27064326, PMID: 22009916). Some evidence is only partial: the CLEAR study found no overall VA improvement in the lutein group but a significant between-group difference and benefit in a worse-baseline subgroup (PMID: 23385792), while another placebo-controlled trial showed only a trend toward BCVA improvement despite visual-function benefits (PMID: 22858124). There are also discrepancies in this contradicting set: one study used a 'faux placebo' that still contained lutein, limiting its relevance to a true placebo comparison (PMID: 22027699), and one recent report lacked a placebo control and mainly suggested stabilization rather than clear acuity improvement (PMID: 37637949). The main theme is that several controlled studies, including some placebo-controlled trials, do report visual-acuity benefit, but interpretation is tempered because many interventions combined lutein with zeaxanthin, omega-3s, antioxidants, or other nutrients.
conclusion: The statement is overall contradicted. Although several studies show no clear visual-acuity improvement despite increases in macular pigment, multiple placebo-controlled and randomized trials report statistically significant visual-acuity benefits with lutein-containing supplementation, and this contradicting evidence is both more direct and generally stronger.

claim: In patients with age-related macular degeneration, lutein supplementation does not produce a clear improvement in macular function measured by microperimetry compared with placebo, although greater increases in macular pigment optical density are associated with greater improvements in visual function.
support_summary: Supporting evidence is limited and mostly indirect. A randomized trial in age-related macular disease found no significant benefit of lutein-containing supplementation on contrast sensitivity versus placebo, suggesting no clear functional improvement, although it did not use microperimetry or assess MPOD-function correlations (PMID: 17268417). Two other randomized studies reported no significant rise in MPOD after lutein/zeaxanthin supplementation, but one involved offspring of AMD patients without retinal disease and the other focused on MPOD measurement rather than macular function, so their relevance to the specific claim is limited (PMID: 28973076; PMID: 28336284). Overall, these studies align with the “no clear improvement” part of the claim more than the MPOD-visual function association, and there is a discrepancy because some are misaligned with the exact AMD/microperimetry question.
contradict_summary: Most contradicting evidence comes from randomized controlled trials in early/nonadvanced AMD showing benefit of lutein-containing supplementation on retinal function or visual performance. Most directly, a placebo-controlled trial reported significant increases in microperimetry-determined mean retinal sensitivity after 10 or 20 mg lutein, with no such increase in placebo, directly contradicting the claim’s statement that microperimetry shows no clear improvement; the same study also found increased MPOD (PMID: 25228440). Related trials found improved mfERG central responses and positive correlations between MPOD increases and improved retinal responses or contrast sensitivity/visual acuity, supporting a mechanistic link between MPOD rise and visual benefit rather than a null effect (PMID: 22835510; PMID: 22858124; PMID: 25815324). Additional studies, though often using other outcomes or combined formulations rather than microperimetry alone, also reported improvements in BCVA, contrast sensitivity, retinal sensitivity, or electrophysiology with lutein-containing supplementation versus placebo/no treatment (PMID: 15117055; PMID: 23385792; PMID: 23695657; PMID: 17716735; PMID: 12511345). Some contradiction items are only indirectly relevant because they lacked placebo, used mixed nutrients, or did not assess microperimetry, but the overall theme strongly favors functional benefit rather than no effect.
conclusion: The statement is overall more contradicted than supported. The strongest and most directly relevant evidence is a randomized placebo-controlled trial showing improved microperimetry-measured retinal sensitivity with lutein in early AMD (PMID: 25228440), while the supporting studies are fewer and mostly indirect or do not address the exact microperimetry-based question.

claim: In patients with neovascular age-related macular degeneration, ranibizumab and bevacizumab produced similar improvements in visual acuity over a 2-year period.
support_summary: Multiple direct comparative studies and reviews report little to no difference in 2-year visual acuity outcomes between ranibizumab and bevacizumab for neovascular AMD. The strongest evidence comes from randomized trials: the LUCAS treat-and-extend trial found equivalent 2-year letter gains (7.4 vs 6.6 letters) with no significant difference (PMID: 26477842), and the IVAN trial reported similar 2-year efficacy with a mean difference of -1.37 letters between drugs (PMID: 23870813; detailed report PMID: 26445075). Meta-analyses likewise found no significant difference in BCVA at 2 years between the drugs (PMID: 25709924; PMID: 24790885). Some supporting items are weaker or only indirectly relevant because they involve 1-year follow-up, mixed regimens, observational data, or combination therapy rather than a clean 2-year head-to-head comparison (for example PMID: 22553617, PMID: 23292928, PMID: 27433034), but overall the recurring theme is comparable visual acuity benefit.
contradict_summary: Most contradicting items do not directly refute the claim's core statement about average 2-year visual acuity improvement; instead, they raise subgroup, switching, regimen, or morphology differences. Several retrospective switch/refractory studies suggest some patients may do worse after switching from ranibizumab to bevacizumab or may respond better to ranibizumab when bevacizumab-refractory (PMID: 25196907, PMID: 24311928, PMID: 36633668), but these are selected populations and not direct tests of initial 2-year equivalence. A notable CATT subgroup analysis found sustained visual acuity loss was somewhat more likely with bevacizumab (OR 1.83) despite such loss being relatively rare overall (PMID: 24875610), and morphology analyses reported ranibizumab monthly resolved retinal fluid better, without directly showing superior overall 2-year visual acuity gains (PMID: 26783095). Some items appear miscategorized as contradiction because they are cost-effectiveness reviews, opinion pieces, or analyses of regimen effects rather than evidence of different 2-year visual acuity improvement between the two drugs (for example PMID: 24405740, PMID: 24167325, PMID: 21961847, PMID: 23642785).
conclusion: The statement is overall more supported than contradicted. High-quality 2-year randomized trial evidence and meta-analyses consistently indicate similar mean visual acuity improvement with ranibizumab and bevacizumab, whereas most contrary items concern selected subgroups, treatment switching, or indirect outcomes rather than direct contradiction of overall 2-year visual acuity equivalence.

claim: In patients with neovascular age-related macular degeneration, as-needed dosing produced less improvement in visual acuity than monthly dosing, whether started at treatment initiation or after switching from monthly treatment.
support_summary: Several randomized trials and analyses indicate that less frequent or PRN/discontinuous anti-VEGF dosing can yield smaller visual-acuity gains than regular/monthly dosing in neovascular AMD. Direct evidence includes GMAN, where routine bevacizumab outperformed PRN dosing (5.5 vs 0.6 letters at 92 weeks; PMID: 25892016), pooled ranibizumab trial analyses showing more ≥15-letter responders with continued monthly dosing than with switching to less frequent regimens after loading (PMID: 23251787), and DRAGON, where monthly ranibizumab produced greater BCVA gains than early PRN dosing through 12 and 24 months (PMID: 33377611). Reviews/meta-analyses and longer-term trial reports also support a modest efficacy loss with discontinuous/PRN treatment, including IVAN 2-year results (small loss with discontinuous treatment; PMID: 23870813), CATT-related review findings that 2-year PRN outcomes were lower than monthly outcomes (PMID: 23642785), and a meta-analysis concluding monthly dosing was superior to PRN for BCVA and CRT (PMID: 25709924). Evidence addressing switching from regular treatment to less intensive regimens also generally supports the claim, with declines or less stable vision after transition to PRN reported in VIEW/TREX analyses (PMID: 26477840; PMID: 28779006), though several support items are indirect because they compare PRN with treat-and-extend or treatment regularity rather than strictly monthly dosing.
contradict_summary: Important contradictory evidence comes from major randomized trials showing that PRN/discontinuous or treat-and-extend regimens can achieve visual outcomes comparable or noninferior to monthly dosing, especially at 1 year. In CATT, ranibizumab PRN with monthly monitoring was equivalent to monthly ranibizumab at 1 year (PMID: 21526923), and IVAN reported equivalent visual acuity for discontinuous versus continuous treatment at 1 year (PMID: 22578446). Multiple treat-and-extend studies found noninferior or comparable BCVA gains versus monthly ranibizumab, including CANTREAT at 12 and 24 months (PMID: 30677465; PMID: 31917441), TREND (PMID: 28893454), and TREX-AMD at 1 and 2 years (PMID: 26391465; PMID: 31047517), though these regimens are not identical to PRN and thus are a notable categorization discrepancy relative to the claim. Additional contradictions come from HARBOR, where monthly and PRN ranibizumab groups had clinically meaningful and similar 24-month gains (PMID: 25015215), and from studies of nonmonthly continuous delivery or extended fixed regimens showing efficacy comparable to monthly dosing (e.g., aflibercept q8w after loading, PMID: 23084240; port delivery system trials, PMID: 34597713; PMID: 30946888), which are only partially relevant because they are not classic as-needed dosing.
conclusion: The statement is overall somewhat more contradicted than supported. Although several studies and reviews suggest PRN/discontinuous dosing often gives slightly worse visual outcomes—especially over longer follow-up and after switching from regular treatment—the strongest contradictory evidence includes multiple randomized noninferiority trials showing PRN or treat-and-extend approaches can match monthly dosing, and many cited support items are indirect or not strictly monthly-vs-PRN comparisons.

claim: In patients with neovascular age-related macular degeneration, ranibizumab and bevacizumab had similar rates of death and arteriothrombotic events, but bevacizumab was associated with more systemic serious adverse events.
support_summary: The strongest direct support comes from the CATT randomized trial, which reported similar rates of death, myocardial infarction, and stroke for bevacizumab and ranibizumab, but a higher proportion of serious systemic adverse events with bevacizumab (24.1% vs 19.0%) (PMID: 21526923). Two meta-analyses also found no significant difference in death or arterial thrombotic events, while reporting higher rates of systemic serious adverse events or broader MedDRA system-organ-class events with bevacizumab (PMID: 25709924, PMID: 24790885). Additional review/commentary papers based largely on CATT noted a higher serious systemic adverse-event signal with bevacizumab but emphasized uncertainty and need for further study rather than definitive causation (PMID: 21970950, PMID: 23642785). One exploratory IVAN analysis suggested bevacizumab increased hazard of immunologically mediated events, which is compatible with more systemic adverse events, though it does not directly establish similar death rates and is narrower than the full claim (PMID: 30555977). Some included support items are indirect or not head-to-head with ranibizumab, such as a bevacizumab-versus-control study, so their relevance to the exact claim is limited (PMID: 23569355).
contradict_summary: Several head-to-head randomized trials found no significant difference in serious adverse events between bevacizumab and ranibizumab, directly challenging the claim that bevacizumab causes more systemic serious adverse events; this was reported in IVAN 1-year, GEFAL, LUCAS 2-year, BRAMD, MANTA, and a small Turkish comparative study (PMID: 22578446, PMID: 23916488, PMID: 26477842, PMID: 27203434, PMID: 23292928, PMID: 24558597). IVAN 1-year even reported fewer arteriothrombotic events or heart failure with bevacizumab, while LUCAS 1-year reported fewer arteriothrombotic events in the bevacizumab group, which conflicts with the 'similar rates' portion of the claim (PMID: 22578446, PMID: 25227499). Larger observational and database studies generally found no significant differences between drugs in stroke, myocardial infarction, venous thromboembolism, hospitalization, mortality, or related systemic outcomes (PMID: 22763393, PMID: 23255862, PMID: 32781110, PMID: 30069864). An individual-patient-data meta-analysis of 5 trials found no large differences for at least one serious adverse event, death, or arteriothrombotic events between bevacizumab and ranibizumab (PMID: 29038796). Notably, one item placed in the contradict group actually partly aligns with the claim by stating bevacizumab had a statistically higher observed risk of serious adverse events, though cardiovascular event differences were not apparent, indicating some categorization noise (PMID: 21961847).
conclusion: The claim is overall mixed but somewhat more contradicted than supported. Although CATT and some meta-analyses support similar death/arteriothrombotic event rates with more systemic serious adverse events for bevacizumab, multiple other randomized trials, a large individual-patient-data meta-analysis, and observational studies failed to confirm an excess of systemic serious adverse events and often found broadly similar safety profiles.
